purpose.question: What is the purpose of this solution?
purpose.answer: A Product / Development / QA workspace: the Rust desktop application teams use to run the CooperativeAICoding loop. The main window has a top menu with four tabs — Product, Develop, Test, Admin — each with its own colour; clicking a tab enters that environment. Product plans products (Strategy with Deliverables, a work-item hierarchy with commercial fields, drag-and-drop feature design, an AI button that generates the work achieving a Deliverable, specs that generate endpoints/front-end/database designs); Develop builds them (Technical Strategy, Board/Sprint/List views filtered by user, Solutions linked to Products and to GitHub repositories, code editor, real embedded terminal, multi-repository support, AI via API keys with per-work-item and per-Product policies); Test designs QA tests (Testing Strategy plus test cases associated with Deliverables or work items) that AI can implement; Admin names team members, assigns roles, and decides which areas and commercial fields each role sees. There are still no logins — roles organise the workspace, they do not secure it.

hosting.question: Where will it be hosted, deployed, or distributed?
hosting.answer: A downloadable native executable for Windows and Linux; no server-side hosting. Coded for low memory usage and good performance on low-spec machines.

frameworks.question: What frameworks, libraries, or UI toolkit should it use?
frameworks.answer: Tauri for the GUI shell. The frontend is React 19 + Vite + TypeScript: Monaco (VS Code's editor component) for the code editor, dnd-kit for the drag-and-drop feature-design canvas, and xterm.js for the terminal view. The in-app terminal is a real embedded shell — a genuine OS shell process (via a PTY crate such as portable-pty) rendered inside the Tauri window via xterm.js — not a simulated command box and not a text-mode UI crate like ratatui, which can't render inside a GUI webview.

authType.question: How does this solution authenticate or authorise callers/users?
authType.answer: None — single-user local desktop app; no login, no user accounts. The app opens straight into the workspace.

rolesOrClaims.question: Does it authorise by Claims or Roles?
rolesOrClaims.answer: Roles, but not as an authorisation boundary. There is still no login, so nothing is authenticated and anyone can switch who they are working as — roles decide which of the four tabs are shown and whether a work item's cost/profit/chargeable fields are visible, and that is all. Seeded roles (Admin, Product, Developer, QA) are editable in the Admin area; Admin cannot be deleted or weakened, and with no active member selected everything is visible so the user cannot lock themselves out.

lookAndFeel.question: What is the overall look and feel?
lookAndFeel.answer: Minimal and easy to use. A top menu with four tabs — Product, Develop, Test, Admin — each with its own colour identifying its environment; clicking a tab enters that environment. A "Working as…" picker sits beside the tabs and sets the active team member, which decides the tabs and commercial fields on show.

stylingOrToolkit.question: What styling approach, component library, or UI toolkit?
stylingOrToolkit.answer: Plain CSS with CSS-variable theming: one variable set per workspace tab colour plus the user-customisable colours. No CSS framework — keeps the dependency footprint small for low-spec machines.

interactionModel.question: How do people interact with it?
interactionModel.answer: Both — typed commands in a terminal, and mouse drag-and-drop to move code blocks or UI designs around.

configLocation.question: Where does it read/write its own config and data on disk?
configLocation.answer: To the local database file (CoperativeAIdb) in the OS-appropriate default app-data folder, overridable by an environment variable.

errorLogging.question: How do we log errors and issues?
errorLogging.answer: To the local database file (CoperativeAIdb) in the OS-appropriate default app-data folder, overridable by an environment variable.

documentation.question: How is this solution documented for its users or callers?

boilerplate.question: Start from a named boilerplate?
boilerplate.note: Corrected from 'rust-cli-app' during the first build attempt — this solution is a real windowed GUI (Tauri) with an embedded terminal, which needs the src-tauri/ + frontend layout, not a plain cargo-new binary. See boilerplates.json's own note on the difference.

fileLayout.question: What is the folder and file layout of this solution's repo?
answer: app/CoperativeAI/src/pages/ — one component per page brief (workspace shell, product planning, editor, terminal, …)
app/CoperativeAI/src/lib/ — shared frontend logic (Tauri command wrappers, theming)
app/CoperativeAI/src-tauri/src/db/ — all turso database access (one module per table)

testing.question: What test framework should be used, and what kinds of tests?
testing.answer: TDD per the Project Brief's dev-rules: always write a failing test first, then just enough code to pass it. Tests start simple and grow more complex as functionality is added.

commands.question: The commands the AI should use to work with this repo.
commands.install: Install Rust, Cargo, and Node; run `cargo fetch` in src-tauri/ and `npm install` for the frontend.
commands.run: `npm run tauri dev` to start the application in development mode.
commands.build: `npm run tauri build` to produce release binaries for Windows and Linux.
commands.deploy: N/A — no server-side deployment. Release binaries are produced by the pipeline and published as downloadable artifacts.
commands.test: `cargo test` from src-tauri/ for backend logic; `npm test` (Vitest) for the frontend.

scaffold.note: Based on the tauri-desktop-app boilerplate; the testing answer above overrides the boilerplate's testing entry. Commands were originally written assuming a plain cargo binary — corrected here to match Tauri's actual install/run/build/test shape during the first build attempt.

baseline.question: Start from a named security baseline?

rules.question: Any extra security rules for this solution?
entries: Any file dragged in or opened by the user is validated (path, type, size) before being read.

AI provider API keys are stored in the OS credential store (Windows Credential Manager / Linux Secret Service) via a Tauri keyring plugin — never in plaintext in the database, config files, code, or logs. The database stores only a key alias.
Per-work-item AI policies are enforced deny-by-default: before any work-item content is sent to an AI provider, the item's policy must explicitly allow that use and that provider.
The embedded terminal runs as a real shell with the same permissions as the OS user running the app — no privilege escalation, and no separate elevated process.
The embedded terminal is local only — never exposed over a network socket or remote-accessible interface.
Output copied from or displayed in the embedded terminal is never logged or persisted by the application itself.

resources.question: What infrastructure resources does this solution need?

provisioning.question: How is infrastructure created, and by whom?
provisioning.answer: N/A — no server-side infrastructure. Release binaries are produced by the pipeline and published as downloadable artifacts.

environments.question: What environments exist, and which may the AI deploy to?
environments.answer: Development (AI may build and deploy debug builds), production (people deploy after review) — performance-focused release builds.

settings.question: What named settings and secrets does this solution need at build or run time?

entries.name: COPERATIVEAI_DATA_DIR
entries.whatItIs: Where the app stores its local data, including the embedded CoperativeAIdb database file
entries.where: OS-appropriate default app-data folder, overridable by an environment variable

pipeline.question: What CI/CD pipeline should this solution have?
pipeline.answer: GitHub Actions: on PR → cargo fetch, cargo build, cargo test; on merge to main → build release binaries for Windows and Linux and publish as artifacts for the target environment.

mustNotDo.question: Anything this solution must NOT do / cannot deploy?

limits.question: Any limits (rate limits, payload size, memory, startup time, supported platforms)?
limits.answer: Low memory usage and good performance on low-spec Windows and Linux machines (from the Project Brief).

openQuestions: The embedded terminal is a real shell with full OS-user permissions — should it be restricted in any way (e.g. confined to the project's working directory, or a denylist of commands), or is full shell access intended?
Any must-not-do constraints for this solution specifically (beyond the project-wide rules)?
